COS10004 Computer Systems
               Lecture 3.5: Shift Registers







Dr Chris McCarthy

              SHIFT REGISTERS
        A shift register takes input from one end, and at each clock
           change this value is moved to the next D-Flip-Flop.
        This is used in serial data transfer when a byte (say) of data
           sent on a cable one bit after another can be collected in a
           series of D Flip-Flops to rebuild the whole data byte. This is
           called serial-to-parallel conversion.














2                               COS10004 Computer Systems                                   2

    SHIFT REGISTERS

Here is how a high                              A                B                 C
travels through a 3 bit                           Q                Q                 Q   Output
shift register. For this          Input      D                 D                D
example we assume                                 Q                Q                 Q
that each of the shift
register bits is cleared at                   Clock            Clock             Clock
the start.

 3                                  COS10004 Computer Systems                                     3

            SHIFT LEFT SHIFT REGISTERS
  >‚ÄĮ The shift registers shown so far shift data to the
     right. A simple rewiring gives a shift register that
     can move data left. Of course these may also have
     the ability to parallel load.



















4                            COS10004 Computer Systems                            4

 WHAT DOES A SHIFT REGISTER DO?

>‚ÄĮ Moves a state (number such as 0, 1) from a low order bit
   to a higher order bit.
>‚ÄĮ Multiplies a (binary) number by two.
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Number of "shifts" depends on number of clock cycles.
>‚ÄĮ Level counter (e.g. volume control)
>‚ÄĮ Can use a counter to enable/disable clock, thereby
   programming the amount of shift.
>‚ÄĮ Can shift in the other direction - divide numbers by 2.
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Can have two clocks - one for left-shift, one for right-
        shift, or use gates to determine the shift direction.
>‚ÄĮ A 1-bit stack ‚Äď we‚Äôll come back to this                                    5

              SERIAL-TO-PARALLEL CONVERSION


      >‚ÄĮ  Some shift registers allow all flip-flops to load at once,
      >‚ÄĮ  i.e. in parallel.









      >‚ÄĮ  This gives parallel-to-serial conversion


6                               COS10004 Computer Systems                                   6

RIGHT-SHIFT SERIAL OR PARALLEL INPUT: 4-BIT














                           Parallel Output

           >‚ÄĮ  Flip-flops are connected (output to input) with a common
               clock to cascade input from high bytes to low bytes.
           >‚ÄĮ  Each flip-flop has a parallel bit OR-ed to the input to allow
               PReset.                                                                7

WHAT ABOUT BI-DIRECTIONAL SHIFTING (SELECTABLE) ?


>‚ÄĮ This takes some thinking!
>‚ÄĮ You need a pin to select which direction
>‚ÄĮ You need to allow inputs to any D Flip Flop to come from
   either direction
>‚ÄĮ Try and design a 2 bit selectable direction Shift Register !
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Serial input only
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ You will probably need some OR gates ,AND gates,
        and a NOT gate




                                                                         8

THE LOGISIM SHIFT REGISTER
 (NOT FOR USE IN ASSIGNMENTS!

  THINGS TO TRY
>‚ÄĮ Parallel Load a value and Shift to see it halve.
>‚ÄĮ Implement both directions and see it double or halve.
>‚ÄĮ Hold down SI to inject a bit - see it halve or double.

  WEEK 3 SUMMARY
>‚ÄĮ Counters:
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Ripple counter (asynchronous)
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Common clock counters (synchronised)
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Mod 6 counter
          ‚ÄĘ‚ÄĮ Use logic to detect illegal state and reset FFs
          ‚ÄĘ‚ÄĮ Use D FFs to buffer output and stabilise
     ‚Äď‚ÄĮ Shift Registers with connected D FFs
          ‚ÄĘ‚ÄĮ Extremely useful for multi/div, bit shifting, level
             setting, serial/parallel data conversion.
